title: Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle
subtitle: [Coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching) typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat.  This is more likely during El Niño stages of the [ENSO cycle](#dod:enso_cycle) when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.
caption:
Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle

Coral bleaching typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat.  This is more likely during El Niño stages of the ENSO cycle when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.

This bar chart tracks the annual count of coral bleaching events from 1980 through 2016, categorizing each year by the prevailing phase of the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) cycle. The vertical axis measures the number of events, ranging from 0 to 80, while the horizontal axis marks the timeline. A legend distinguishes three categories: El Niño years in dark red, Moderate years in purple, and La Niña years in blue. The data shows a clear upward trend in the frequency of events over the four decades, with the highest bars appearing in the later years of the timeline. El Niño years consistently produce the largest spikes in activity; the year 1998 stands as the absolute maximum on the chart, recording over 70 events. Other significant peaks occur during El Niño phases in 2016, with roughly 55 events, and 1987, with approximately 25 events. La Niña years also show substantial activity, particularly in 2010, which reaches over 50 events, and 2008, which sits near 15. Moderate years generally display lower counts, though 2005 reaches 40 events and 2003 exceeds 20. The early 1980s show minimal activity across all categories, with values mostly under 10. The distribution of bar heights indicates that while bleaching occurs during all phases, the magnitude of events is most pronounced during El Niño conditions and has increased in frequency since the turn of the millennium. The tallest bar on the chart corresponds to the 1998 El Niño event.
